entries.text:
Blood Glucose Targets for Adults with Diabetes (ADA Guidelines)

For most non-pregnant adults with diabetes, the American Diabetes Association recommends:
- Before meals (fasting/preprandial): 80-130 mg/dL
- 2 hours after meals (postprandial): Less than 180 mg/dL
- HbA1c target: Less than 7% for most adults

Hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) is defined as blood glucose below 70 mg/dL. Severe hypoglycemia occurs below 54 mg/dL and requires immediate treatment.

Hyperglycemia (high blood sugar) occur

treatment.

Hyperglycemia (high blood sugar) occurs when blood glucose exceeds 180 mg/dL after meals or 130 mg/dL fasting.

Signs of hypoglycemia: shakiness, sweating, confusion, rapid heartbeat, hunger, dizziness. Treat with 15g fast-acting carbohydrates (e.g., glucose tablets, 4 oz juice).

Signs of hyperglycemia: frequent urination, increased thirst, fatigue, blurred vision, headache.

The 15-15 rule for mild hypoglycemia: consume 15g carbohydrates, wait 15 minutes, recheck blood sugar. Repea

rates, wait 15 minutes, recheck blood sugar. Repeat if still below 70 mg/dL.

Always consult your healthcare provider to determine your individual blood glucose targets.

Carbohydrate Counting for Diabetes Management

Carbohydrate counting (carb counting) is a meal planning tool that helps people with diabetes manage blood glucose levels. Carbohydrates have the most direct effect on blood sugar.

What counts as a carbohydrate serving?
- 1 serving = 15 grams of carbohydrates
- Most people with diabetes aim for 45-60 grams of carbs per meal (3-4 servings)
- Snacks: 15-30 grams (1-2 servings)

High-carb foods to monitor:
- Grains: bread (1 slice = ~15g), rice (1/3 c

tor:
- Grains: bread (1 slice = ~15g), rice (1/3 cup cooked = 15g), pasta (1/3 cup cooked = 15g)
- Starchy vegetables: potato (1/2 cup = ~15g), corn (1/2 cup = ~15g)
- Fruits: apple (1 small = ~15g), banana (1/2 medium = ~15g), grapes (17 small = ~15g)
- Dairy: milk (1 cup = ~12g), yogurt (varies, check label)
- Legumes: beans (1/3 cup = ~15g), lentils (1/4 cup = ~15g)
- Sweets: cookies, cake, candy, sweetened beverages

Low-carb foods (less than 5g per serving, usually free foods):
- Non-starch

5g per serving, usually free foods):
- Non-starchy vegetables: broccoli, lettuce, cucumber, peppers, cauliflower, spinach
- Meat, poultry, fish, eggs (no carbs)
- Fats: oils, butter, nuts (small amounts)
- Most cheeses

Glycemic Index (GI): Foods with high GI raise blood sugar faster. Prefer low-GI options like whole grains, legumes, and non-starchy vegetables.

The glycemic load (GL) considers both GI and the amount of carbohydrate in a portion.

Always work with a registered dietitian to crea

.

Always work with a registered dietitian to create a personalized meal plan.

Common Diabetes Medications

NON-INSULIN MEDICATIONS FOR TYPE 2 DIABETES:

METFORMIN (Glucophage):
- First-line medication for type 2 diabetes
- Mechanism: reduces glucose production in the liver, improves insulin sensitivity
- Benefits: weight neutral/modest weight loss, low hypoglycemia risk, cardioprotective, affordable
- Side effects: GI upset (nausea, diarrhea) — take with food; rare risk of lactic acidosis
- Take with meals

SGLT2 INHIBITORS (Empagliflozin/Jardiance, Dapagliflozin/Farxiga,

S (Empagliflozin/Jardiance, Dapagliflozin/Farxiga, Canagliflozin/Invokana):
- Mechanism: cause kidneys to excrete excess glucose in urine
- Benefits: cardiovascular and kidney protection, weight loss, blood pressure reduction
- Side effects: genital yeast infections, UTIs, increased urination, rare DKA risk
- Take in the morning

GLP-1 RECEPTOR AGONISTS (Semaglutide/Ozempic/Wegovy, Liraglutide/Victoza, Dulaglutide/Trulicity):
- Mechanism: mimic GLP-1 hormone — stimulate insulin, suppress glucago

LP-1 hormone — stimulate insulin, suppress glucagon, slow gastric emptying
- Benefits: significant weight loss, cardiovascular protection, once weekly dosing available
- Side effects: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea (usually improves), rare pancreatitis
- Injectable (semaglutide also available as oral pill Rybelsus)

DPP-4 INHIBITORS (Sitagliptin/Januvia, Saxagliptin/Onglyza, Alogliptin/Nesina):
- Mechanism: increase incretin levels, stimulate insulin release
- Benefits: weight neutral, low hypoglyce

release
- Benefits: weight neutral, low hypoglycemia risk, well tolerated
- Side effects: nasopharyngitis, joint pain, rare pancreatitis

SULFONYLUREAS (Glipizide, Glimepiride, Glyburide):
- Mechanism: stimulate pancreas to produce more insulin
- Benefits: inexpensive, effective, long track record
- Side effects: hypoglycemia (significant risk), weight gain

THIAZOLIDINEDIONES (Pioglitazone/Actos):
- Mechanism: improve insulin sensitivity in fat and muscle
- Benefits: durable effect, may protec

and muscle
- Benefits: durable effect, may protect pancreatic beta cells
- Side effects: weight gain, fluid retention, increased fracture risk, bladder cancer risk (pioglitazone)

Always take medications as prescribed. Never stop or change doses without consulting your healthcare provider.

Exercise and Diabetes Management

Physical activity is a cornerstone of diabetes management. Regular exercise improves insulin sensitivity, helps control blood glucose, and reduces cardiovascular risk.

ADA RECOMMENDATIONS:
- 150+ minutes per week of moderate-to-vigorous aerobic activity
- Resistance exercise 2-3 times per week
- No more than 2 consecutive days without aerobic exercise
- Break up prolonged sitting every 30 minutes

EFFECTS ON BLOOD GLUCOSE:
- Aerobic exercise generally LOWERS bl

OD GLUCOSE:
- Aerobic exercise generally LOWERS blood glucose (muscles use glucose for energy)
- High-intensity/anaerobic exercise can temporarily RAISE blood glucose (stress hormones)
- Effects can last 24-48 hours post-exercise

BLOOD GLUCOSE MANAGEMENT AROUND EXERCISE:
- Check blood glucose before, during (if >30 min), and after exercise
- Safe range to exercise: 100-250 mg/dL (consult your doctor for your targets)
- Below 100 mg/dL: eat 15-30g carbs before exercise
- Above 250 mg/dL with ket

g carbs before exercise
- Above 250 mg/dL with ketones: postpone exercise
- Above 300 mg/dL: use caution, postpone until glucose is controlled

HYPOGLYCEMIA DURING EXERCISE:
- More common with type 1 diabetes
- Carry fast-acting carbohydrates (glucose tablets, juice)
- May need to reduce insulin dose before exercise (consult provider)
- Post-exercise hypoglycemia can occur hours later — monitor overnight

BEST TYPES OF EXERCISE:
- Walking (most accessible, consistently beneficial)
- Swimming, cy

ccessible, consistently beneficial)
- Swimming, cycling (low impact, good for joint issues)
- Strength training (increases muscle mass, improves insulin sensitivity)
- Yoga (also reduces stress, which affects blood sugar)

Always consult your healthcare provider before starting a new exercise program.

Diabetes and Foot Care

People with diabetes are at higher risk for foot problems due to nerve damage (neuropathy) and poor circulation (peripheral artery disease). Foot complications are the leading cause of non-traumatic lower limb amputations.

DAILY FOOT CARE ROUTINE:
1. Inspect feet daily — check all surfaces, between toes, for cuts, blisters, redness, swelling, or nail problems
2. Wash feet daily in warm (not hot) water — test water temperature with elbow
3. Dry thoroughly, especially betw

ture with elbow
3. Dry thoroughly, especially between toes
4. Moisturize — apply lotion to top and bottom but NOT between toes (prevents fungal infections)
5. Never walk barefoot, even indoors
6. Trim toenails straight across, file edges — don't cut corners
7. Wear clean, dry, well-fitting socks (seamless preferred) and proper footwear

CHOOSING FOOTWEAR:
- Wear shoes that fit well with adequate toe room
- Break in new shoes gradually
- Check inside shoes for foreign objects before putting on
-

ide shoes for foreign objects before putting on
- Consider diabetic/therapeutic footwear if recommended by your doctor

WARNING SIGNS — SEE A DOCTOR PROMPTLY:
- Any cut, sore, or wound that doesn't heal within a few days
- Ingrown toenails or toenail infections
- Calluses or corns (don't try to remove at home)
- Redness, warmth, or swelling
- Numbness, tingling, or pain in feet/legs
- Color changes (pale, bluish, or dark discoloration)
- Any blister, even painless

DIABETIC NEUROPATHY:
- Periphe

ter, even painless

DIABETIC NEUROPATHY:
- Peripheral neuropathy can cause loss of sensation — you may not feel injuries
- Symptoms: numbness, tingling, burning, or shooting pain, usually starting in toes
- Autonomic neuropathy can cause dry skin (increased injury risk)

CLINICAL FOOT EXAMS:
- Complete foot examination at every primary care visit (for those with complications)
- Annual comprehensive foot exam including monofilament test for sensation

Never use heating pads, hot water bottles, o

tion

Never use heating pads, hot water bottles, or electric blankets on your feet. Never use chemical corn/callus removers.

HbA1c (Hemoglobin A1c) Explained

HbA1c measures your average blood glucose over the past 2-3 months. It reflects the percentage of hemoglobin proteins in your blood that have glucose attached.

HbA1c ranges:
- Normal (no diabetes): Below 5.7%
- Prediabetes: 5.7% to 6.4%
- Diabetes diagnosis: 6.5% or higher
- Well-controlled diabetes target: Below 7% (for most adults)
- Higher target (less stringent): 7-8% (for elderly, limited life expectancy, or frequent hypoglycemia)

Approximate average bloo

r frequent hypoglycemia)

Approximate average blood glucose by HbA1c:
- 6%   → ~126 mg/dL
- 7%   → ~154 mg/dL
- 8%   → ~183 mg/dL
- 9%   → ~212 mg/dL
- 10%  → ~240 mg/dL
- 11%  → ~269 mg/dL
- 12%  → ~298 mg/dL

HbA1c is typically measured every 3 months in people not meeting treatment goals, and every 6 months in those who are stable and meeting goals.

Limitations: HbA1c may not accurately reflect glucose control in people with certain conditions (sickle cell disease, hemolytic anemia, recent b

s (sickle cell disease, hemolytic anemia, recent blood transfusion, iron deficiency anemia).

Always consult your healthcare provider for interpretation of your HbA1c results.

Insulin Types and How They Work

Insulin is a hormone that allows glucose to enter cells for energy. People with diabetes may need insulin therapy when the body doesn't produce enough.

RAPID-ACTING INSULIN:
- Examples: Lispro (Humalog), Aspart (NovoLog), Glulisine (Apidra), Fiasp
- Onset: 10-30 minutes
- Peak: 1-2 hours
- Duration: 3-5 hours
- Use: taken at mealtime to cover carbohydrates, or to correct high blood sugar

SHORT-ACTING INSULIN (Regular):
- Examples: Humulin R, Novolin R
- Onset:

gular):
- Examples: Humulin R, Novolin R
- Onset: 30-60 minutes
- Peak: 2-4 hours
- Duration: 5-8 hours
- Use: taken 30-45 minutes before meals

INTERMEDIATE-ACTING INSULIN (NPH):
- Examples: Humulin N, Novolin N
- Onset: 1-3 hours
- Peak: 4-12 hours
- Duration: 12-18 hours
- Use: once or twice daily background insulin

LONG-ACTING INSULIN:
- Examples: Glargine (Lantus, Basaglar), Detemir (Levemir), Degludec (Tresiba)
- Onset: 1-2 hours
- No pronounced peak
- Duration: 20-24 hours (Tresiba: up t

ounced peak
- Duration: 20-24 hours (Tresiba: up to 42 hours)
- Use: once-daily background (basal) insulin

PREMIXED INSULIN:
- Combination of rapid/short-acting and intermediate-acting
- Examples: 70/30, 75/25 formulations
- Less flexibility in dosing

INSULIN DELIVERY:
- Syringes: traditional method
- Insulin pens: more convenient, prefilled cartridges
- Insulin pumps: continuous subcutaneous infusion, most flexible
- Inhaled insulin (Afrezza): rapid-acting, inhaled at meals

Storage: Keep uno

rapid-acting, inhaled at meals

Storage: Keep unopened insulin in the refrigerator (36-46°F). In-use vials/pens can be kept at room temperature (<77°F) for 28-30 days depending on product.

Always consult your healthcare provider or diabetes educator for insulin dosing guidance.

Diabetes-Friendly Meal Planning

Healthy eating is fundamental to diabetes management. There is no single "diabetes diet" — but evidence-based patterns can help manage blood glucose.

EVIDENCE-BASED EATING PATTERNS FOR DIABETES:
1. Mediterranean Diet: rich in vegetables, whole grains, legumes, fish, olive oil; moderate dairy, eggs, poultry; limited red meat and sweets. Reduces cardiovascular risk.
2. DASH Diet: designed to lower blood pressure; high in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, low-fat d

igh in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, low-fat dairy; limits saturated fat, sodium, sweets.
3. Low-Carbohydrate Diet: <130g carbs/day or <26% of calories. Effective for glucose control and weight loss. Requires medication adjustment.
4. Very Low-Carbohydrate/Ketogenic: <50g carbs/day. Very effective short-term; requires close medical supervision.
5. Plant-Based/Vegan: associated with improved glucose control, weight, and cardiovascular health.

THE DIABETES PLATE METHOD (simple approach):
- Fi

THE DIABETES PLATE METHOD (simple approach):
- Fill 1/2 plate with non-starchy vegetables (salad, broccoli, carrots, peppers)
- Fill 1/4 plate with lean protein (chicken, fish, tofu, eggs, beans)
- Fill 1/4 plate with carbohydrate foods (whole grains, starchy vegetables, legumes, fruit)
- Add a small amount of healthy fat
- Drink water or a low-calorie beverage

FOODS TO EMPHASIZE:
- Non-starchy vegetables (unlimited): all leafy greens, cucumber, tomato, pepper, mushrooms, zucchini
- Lean prote

, tomato, pepper, mushrooms, zucchini
- Lean proteins: fish (especially fatty fish twice/week), chicken breast, turkey, eggs, tofu
- Healthy fats: avocado, nuts, seeds, olive oil
- High-fiber whole grains: oatmeal, quinoa, whole wheat, barley
- Legumes: beans, lentils, chickpeas (high fiber, moderate carb)
- Low-glycemic fruits: berries, apple, pear, citrus

FOODS TO LIMIT:
- Sugary beverages (regular soda, juice, energy drinks, sweet tea)
- Refined carbs and white starches (white bread, white r

ned carbs and white starches (white bread, white rice, crackers)
- Processed/ultra-processed foods
- Saturated and trans fats (fried foods, butter, full-fat dairy, processed meats)
- High-sodium foods (raises blood pressure)
- Alcohol (can cause hypoglycemia, especially if on insulin or sulfonylureas)

PRACTICAL TIPS:
- Eat at consistent times each day
- Don't skip meals (especially if on insulin/sulfonylureas)
- Read nutrition labels: look at total carbohydrates, not just sugar
- Eat slowly — i

tal carbohydrates, not just sugar
- Eat slowly — it takes 20 minutes to feel full
- Choose whole foods over processed alternatives

Work with a registered dietitian (RD) or certified diabetes care and education specialist (CDCES) for a personalized meal plan.

Blood Glucose Monitoring and Continuous Glucose Monitors (CGM)

SELF-MONITORING OF BLOOD GLUCOSE (SMBG):
Using a fingerstick glucometer to check blood glucose levels.

When to check (varies by treatment plan):
- Before meals (fasting)
- 2 hours after meals (postprandial)
- Before bedtime
- Before, during, and after exercise
- When feeling symptoms of low or high blood sugar
- Before driving
- At 2-3 AM if nocturnal hypoglycemia is suspected

Glucometer accuracy: should be within 15% or 15 mg/dL

ometer accuracy: should be within 15% or 15 mg/dL of laboratory values. Check calibration, expiration dates, and proper strip storage.

CONTINUOUS GLUCOSE MONITORS (CGM):
CGMs use a small sensor inserted under the skin to measure glucose in interstitial fluid every 1-5 minutes.

MAJOR CGM SYSTEMS:
- Dexcom G7/G6: 10-day wear, real-time readings, integrates with insulin pumps
- FreeStyle Libre 3 (Abbott): 14-day wear, real-time Bluetooth readings, smallest sensor
- Medtronic Guardian: integrates

smallest sensor
- Medtronic Guardian: integrates with Medtronic insulin pumps
- Eversense E3: implantable sensor, 365-day wear (US), physician-inserted

CGM METRICS (Time in Range - TIR):
- Target range for most adults: 70-180 mg/dL
- Goal TIR: >70% of the time
- Time below range (<70 mg/dL): <4% (goal <1% below 54 mg/dL)
- Time above range (>180 mg/dL): <25%

CGM BENEFITS:
- Continuous trend data (not just snapshots)
- Alerts for high/low glucose
- Reduces hypoglycemia risk and anxiety
- Bette

se
- Reduces hypoglycemia risk and anxiety
- Better HbA1c achievement
- Identifies patterns (dawn phenomenon, post-meal spikes)

CGM LIMITATIONS:
- Lag time of 5-15 minutes vs. blood glucose
- May require fingerstick confirmation for treatment decisions (older models)
- Sensor can be inaccurate if placed incorrectly or during compression (sleeping on sensor)
- Cost (insurance coverage varies)

Consult your endocrinologist or diabetes care team about which monitoring approach is right for you.

Sick Day Rules for People with Diabetes

Illness, infection, and stress can raise blood glucose significantly due to stress hormones (cortisol, glucagon). Follow these guidelines during illness.

GENERAL SICK DAY RULES:
1. Never stop taking insulin (even if you're not eating) — you may need MORE
2. Monitor blood glucose every 2-4 hours
3. Check urine or blood ketones if glucose >240 mg/dL (type 1) or >300 mg/dL (type 2)
4. Stay hydrated: aim for 8+ oz fluid per hour
5. Contact your healthcare pr

8+ oz fluid per hour
5. Contact your healthcare provider early

WHEN TO CALL YOUR DOCTOR:
- Blood glucose above 240 mg/dL for more than 2 checks
- Moderate or large ketones in urine
- Unable to keep fluids down for >4 hours (vomiting/diarrhea)
- Illness lasting more than 2 days without improvement
- Signs of dehydration (extreme thirst, dry mouth, dark urine)
- Confusion or difficulty breathing

STAYING HYDRATED WHEN SICK:
- If blood glucose is low or normal: drink regular juice, sports drinks,

ow or normal: drink regular juice, sports drinks, or regular soda
- If blood glucose is high: drink water, broth, diet drinks (no sugar)
- Try to eat 15g carbs every hour if you can't eat regular meals

MEDICATIONS TO AVOID WHEN SICK:
- NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) — can affect kidneys; use acetaminophen instead
- Decongestants can raise blood glucose
- SGLT2 inhibitors — increased DKA risk when sick/not eating; consult provider

KETOACIDOSIS WARNING SIGNS (Call 911 or go to ER):
- Fruity-smelli

NING SIGNS (Call 911 or go to ER):
- Fruity-smelling breath
- Nausea/vomiting
- Abdominal pain
- Rapid or labored breathing
- Confusion or extreme fatigue

Keep a sick day kit: glucose tablets, ketone strips, clear fluids, thermometer, and your healthcare provider's contact information.

Type 1 vs Type 2 Diabetes

TYPE 1 DIABETES:
- Autoimmune condition: the immune system destroys insulin-producing beta cells in the pancreas
- The body produces little or no insulin
- Onset: typically childhood or young adulthood, but can occur at any age
- Treatment: requires insulin therapy (multiple daily injections or insulin pump)
- About 5-10% of all diabetes cases
- Not preventable; not caused by diet or lifestyle
- Symptoms appear quickly: extreme thirst, frequent urination, weight loss,

extreme thirst, frequent urination, weight loss, fatigue, blurred vision

TYPE 2 DIABETES:
- The body doesn't use insulin effectively (insulin resistance) and can't make enough insulin
- Most common form: ~90-95% of all diabetes cases
- Strong association with overweight/obesity, physical inactivity, family history
- Onset: typically adults 45+, but increasingly in younger people
- Treatment: lifestyle changes, oral medications (e.g., metformin), and sometimes insulin
- Can often be prevented o

, and sometimes insulin
- Can often be prevented or delayed with healthy lifestyle
- Symptoms develop gradually; some people have no symptoms initially

GESTATIONAL DIABETES:
- Develops during pregnancy
- Usually resolves after delivery
- Increases risk of type 2 diabetes later in life

PREDIABETES:
- Blood sugar higher than normal but not yet type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 5.7-6.4%)
- Affects ~96 million American adults
- Can often be reversed with lifestyle changes (5-7% weight loss, 150 min/week exer

style changes (5-7% weight loss, 150 min/week exercise)

Consult your healthcare provider for diagnosis and personalized treatment.
